name: Anandavardhana
dates_notes: Ānandavardhana is conventionally placed in ninth-century Kashmir, in the milieu of Avantivarman; the 820-890 range is the standard textual-historical window.
core_thesis: Ānandavardhana is the ninth-century Kashmiri theorist of *dhvani*, poetic suggestion. The *Dhvanyāloka* argues that a poem's highest force is not exhausted by denoted meaning (*abhidhā*), indicated meaning (*lakṣaṇā*), figures, or qualities; the decisive poetic event is suggested meaning (*vyañjanā*), especially the suggestion of *rasa*. The programmatic opening states that learned critics have called *dhvani* the soul of poetry, [*Dhvanyāloka* 1.1](cite://anandavardhana/dhvanyaloka/1.1), *kāvyasyātmā dhvanir iti*. For this site he matters both as a philosopher of language and as the direct object of Abhinavagupta's *Locana*, where dhvani-theory is defended and integrated with Trika-inflected aesthetics. The entry should not overread him as a Vedāntin or Trika author; his own securely attested work is a poetics treatise whose philosophical weight lies in semantic and aesthetic theory.

engaged_works.title: Dhvanyaloka
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Usually placed in Ānandavardhana's ninth-century Kashmiri career.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; the GRETIL text and the later commentarial tradition transmit the work under Ānandavardhana's name.

ascription_evidence.description: Abhinavagupta's Locana comments on the Dhvanyāloka as Ānandavardhana's work.
Modern scholarship treats the Dhvanyāloka as Ānandavardhana's foundational work.

engaged_works.summary: A Sanskrit poetics treatise in four *uddyotas* establishing *dhvani*, suggestion, as the defining excellence of poetry. The first *uddyota* states and defends the thesis that *dhvani* is the soul of poetry against critics who reduce poetic value to denotation, indication, figure, or verbal charm. Later sections classify kinds of suggested meaning and give *rasa-dhvani* a central place. The on-disk GRETIL text includes the root verses and prose exposition, beginning with the programmatic *kāvyasyātmā dhvanir iti*.
